questions.question: Temporal Cloud advertises 'provable security by design' in its whitepaper. What does this mean for your sensitive workflow data?

choices.text: Temporal Cloud stores your workflow data in a separate encrypted vault that only you can unlock with a password.
Temporal Cloud orchestrates your encrypted workflows without ever accessing or reading your sensitive data.
Temporal Cloud audits your workflow data monthly and removes any sensitive fields it detects automatically.
Temporal Cloud shares your workflow data only with compliance-certified third-party security partners.

questions.explanation: The Temporal Cloud Security Whitepaper describes how Temporal Cloud can orchestrate and coordinate workflows without ever being able to read the actual sensitive payload data inside them. This is a core part of Temporal's security value: you can use the platform for durable execution while keeping your business data private through end-to-end encryption, meaning Temporal itself is never in a position to access it.

questions.question: Which security feature is specifically highlighted for Temporal Cloud's SaaS offering to protect data in transit between clients and the Temporal service?

choices.text: End-to-end payload hashing to verify message integrity after each workflow step.
IP allowlists that block all connections from outside your organization's network range.
mTLS (mutual TLS), which requires both the client and server to authenticate each other with certificates.
One-time API tokens that expire after each workflow execution is completed.

questions.explanation: Mutual TLS (mTLS) is called out as a key security feature of Temporal Cloud. Unlike standard TLS where only the server proves its identity, mTLS requires BOTH sides — the client (your Workers or application) and the Temporal server — to present certificates. This prevents unauthorized clients from connecting to your Temporal namespace, which is especially important in a shared SaaS environment.

questions.question: A company wants to run Temporal entirely within its own data center to satisfy internal compliance rules. Which security path does Temporal's documentation point them toward?

choices.text: The Temporal Cloud Security Whitepaper, which details the shared-responsibility model for SaaS tenants.
Company Security at trust.temporal.io, which covers Temporal Technologies' own organizational practices.
The Self-Hosted Security guide, which covers deploying and securing your own Temporal Platform infrastructure.
The Temporal Cloud Security section, since self-hosting and cloud deployments share the same security configuration.

questions.explanation: Temporal offers two deployment models: Temporal Cloud (a managed SaaS service) and self-hosted (running the Temporal server yourself). For teams that need full control over infrastructure — common in regulated industries — the Self-Hosted Security guide provides production-ready best practices for securing your own deployment. Temporal Cloud is a separate product with its own managed security model.

questions.question: Where would you look to find information about Temporal Technologies' compliance certifications and organizational security practices as a company?

choices.text: The Self-Hosted Security guide, which includes vendor certification details alongside deployment instructions.
trust.temporal.io, which covers Temporal Technologies' general security practices and compliance certifications.
The Temporal Cloud Security Whitepaper, which lists all current compliance standards the platform meets.
The Temporal Cloud Security section of the docs, which covers both product and company-level certifications.

questions.explanation: trust.temporal.io is the dedicated resource for information about Temporal Technologies as an organization — things like SOC 2 compliance, security policies, and how the company itself handles security. This is separate from product-level security documentation. When evaluating a vendor, checking a trust/security page like this is standard practice for due diligence.

questions.question: Temporal's security documentation is split into distinct sections for 'Temporal Cloud' and 'Self-Hosted.' Why does this separation make sense from an operator's perspective?

choices.text: The two deployments use completely different programming languages, so the security tooling is unrelated.
Self-hosted deployments are always less secure, so they require a separate guide with additional warnings.
Temporal Cloud and self-hosted share identical configuration files, but the guides are split for marketing reasons.
The two models have different responsibilities: Temporal manages infrastructure security for Cloud, while self-hosters must configure and maintain it themselves.

questions.explanation: In the Temporal Cloud model, Temporal Technologies manages the underlying infrastructure, network security, certificate rotation, and more — you consume security as a managed service. In a self-hosted deployment, your team is responsible for all of that: setting up TLS, managing certificates, securing the database, controlling network access, and so on. The split documentation reflects this real difference in who owns what, a concept known as the shared-responsibility model.

questions.question: End-to-end encryption is listed as a security feature of Temporal Cloud. What problem does this solve that basic transport-layer security (like HTTPS) alone does not?

choices.text: It prevents your workflow code from being copied by other tenants sharing the same Temporal Cloud cluster.
It ensures that workflow payload data remains encrypted even while stored or processed by the Temporal service, so Temporal cannot read it.
It automatically compresses workflow payloads so they transfer faster over encrypted connections.
It rotates your TLS certificates on a fixed schedule so that old credentials cannot be reused by attackers.

questions.explanation: Transport-layer encryption (like HTTPS/TLS) protects data while it travels over the network, but the receiving server can decrypt and read it once it arrives. End-to-end encryption goes further: the payload data is encrypted by your application before it ever reaches Temporal and can only be decrypted by your application on the other end. This means Temporal's infrastructure — even if somehow compromised — never has access to your raw workflow data. This is the foundation of Temporal Cloud's 'provable security by design' claim.

questions.question:
A team runs Temporal Cloud with a custom Data Converter that includes a remote codec server performing AES-256 encryption. Their workers connect via mTLS client certificates. The codec server URL is configured in the Temporal Web UI codec endpoint setting. A security audit finds that workflow history payloads ARE encrypted at rest in Temporal Cloud storage, but the audit team flags that the codec server represents a trust boundary gap. Which statement most accurately describes the actual end-to-end encryption trust model in this configuration?

A) Temporal Cloud never decrypts payload bytes because the codec runs client-side in each worker and in the browser; the mTLS certificates authenticate the worker to Temporal Cloud but carry no payload decryption material, so Temporal's infrastructure is fully excluded from the trust boundary.

B) The mTLS client certificates and the codec server share the same trust boundary because Temporal Cloud's frontend service validates both before routing task queue traffic, meaning a compromised Temporal Cloud operator could correlate certificate identity with decrypted payload content.

C) The Web UI codec endpoint integration means a user's browser calls the codec server directly with encoded payloads; Temporal Cloud itself only ever stores and routes opaque bytes, but the codec server becomes an independent trust boundary that must be hardened separately from the mTLS PKI used for worker connectivity.

D) Because Temporal Cloud stores history events including encoded payloads, enabling the Web UI codec endpoint causes Temporal's servers to proxy decode requests through the codec server during history reads, making Temporal Cloud infrastructure part of the decryption path.

choices.text: Temporal Cloud never decrypts payload bytes because the codec runs client-side in each worker and in the browser; the mTLS certificates authenticate the worker to Temporal Cloud but carry no payload decryption material, so Temporal's infrastructure is fully excluded from the trust boundary.

The mTLS client certificates and the codec server share the same trust boundary because Temporal Cloud's frontend service validates both before routing task queue traffic, meaning a compromised Temporal Cloud operator could correlate certificate identity with decrypted payload content.

The Web UI codec endpoint integration means a user's browser calls the codec server directly with encoded payloads; Temporal Cloud itself only ever stores and routes opaque bytes, but the codec server becomes an independent trust boundary that must be hardened separately from the mTLS PKI used for worker connectivity.

Because Temporal Cloud stores history events including encoded payloads, enabling the Web UI codec endpoint causes Temporal's servers to proxy decode requests through the codec server during history reads, making Temporal Cloud infrastructure part of the decryption path.

questions.explanation: This question exercises the interaction between Temporal Cloud's payload storage model, the Data Converter / codec server pattern, mTLS worker connectivity, and the Web UI codec endpoint. The key architectural insight: Temporal Cloud (and self-hosted clusters) are designed to be 'provably excluded' from payload content. Workers encode payloads before sending them to the server; the server stores, routes, and returns opaque bytes it cannot interpret. When the Web UI codec endpoint is configured, the *browser* makes a direct HTTP(S) call to the operator-run codec server carrying the opaque payload bytes — Temporal's servers are not in this decode path. This is why Temporal's whitepaper claims 'orchestrating encrypted workflows without ever accessing your sensitive data.' The mTLS certs (option A is partially correct) do authenticate workers to Temporal Cloud, but they are orthogonal to payload encryption — they secure the transport channel, not the payload content. Option B is wrong: mTLS and the codec server are independent trust boundaries; Temporal's frontend has no visibility into decoded content. Option D is the dangerous misconception: Temporal's servers never proxy through the codec server. The production implication: your codec server now holds the actual keys to your workflow data. It must have its own auth (e.g., requiring an Authorization header from the browser), rate limiting, audit logging, and availability SLO independent of your Temporal Cloud namespace. A misconfigured or unauthenticated codec server endpoint is a larger blast radius than a compromised mTLS private key, because the cert only lets traffic reach Temporal — the codec server lets you read the data itself.

questions.question:
Your organization self-hosts Temporal and has configured inter-node mTLS with an internal PKI. You are migrating namespaces between two Temporal clusters during a rolling infrastructure upgrade. Workers in the new cluster connect with newly issued mTLS client certificates signed by a rotated intermediate CA. Old workers on the legacy cluster hold certificates from the previous intermediate CA, and both CAs chain to the same root CA. Cross-namespace Nexus endpoints are being used to route calls between a billing namespace (old cluster) and an order namespace (new cluster). Which failure mode is most likely to surface first and why?

A) The Nexus HTTP caller will fail authentication because Nexus endpoint routing evaluates the client certificate's intermediate CA identity against an allowlist per endpoint, and the rotated intermediate CA is not yet registered in the Nexus endpoint's allowed certificate authorities, even though both chain to the same trusted root.

B) Workflow history replay on the new cluster will become non-deterministic because mTLS certificate rotation changes the TLS session identifiers embedded in workflow history events, causing the Temporal SDK's determinism checker to raise errors on activities that completed before the cert rotation.

C) The old cluster's workers will begin receiving TLS handshake failures when polling their task queues because Temporal Server validates the complete certificate chain on every gRPC poll request, and the cached root CA bundle is refreshed only at server restart, leaving the new intermediate CA unrecognized until the old cluster restarts.

D) Workers on both clusters will successfully complete in-progress workflow tasks but new workflow starts routed through Nexus will fail if the Nexus endpoint's target namespace is configured with a fixed cluster affinity that still points to the old cluster's frontend address, which no longer accepts the new intermediate CA.

choices.text: The Nexus HTTP caller will fail authentication because Nexus endpoint routing evaluates the client certificate's intermediate CA identity against an allowlist per endpoint, and the rotated intermediate CA is not yet registered in the Nexus endpoint's allowed certificate authorities, even though both chain to the same trusted root.

Workflow history replay on the new cluster will become non-deterministic because mTLS certificate rotation changes the TLS session identifiers embedded in workflow history events, causing the Temporal SDK's determinism checker to raise errors on activities that completed before the cert rotation.

The old cluster's workers will begin receiving TLS handshake failures when polling their task queues because Temporal Server validates the complete certificate chain on every gRPC poll request, and the cached root CA bundle is refreshed only at server restart, leaving the new intermediate CA unrecognized until the old cluster restarts.

Workers on both clusters will successfully complete in-progress workflow tasks but new workflow starts routed through Nexus will fail if the Nexus endpoint's target namespace is configured with a fixed cluster affinity that still points to the old cluster's frontend address, which no longer accepts the new intermediate CA.

questions.explanation: This question tests the interaction between mTLS certificate rotation, cross-cluster namespace migration, and Nexus endpoint configuration. The core insight: Temporal Server performs mTLS validation at the transport layer using the configured CA bundle (root + intermediates). As long as both intermediate CAs chain to the same trusted root CA and the server's CA bundle includes the root, *both* intermediate CAs will be trusted without requiring per-intermediate registration. This eliminates option A (Nexus does not maintain per-intermediate allowlists separate from the server's TLS config) and option C (the root CA hasn't changed, so existing workers are unaffected). Option B is completely wrong — TLS session state is never written into workflow history events; mTLS is a transport concern entirely separate from the workflow execution layer and has zero effect on replay determinism. Option D captures the real operational failure: Nexus endpoint target addresses are configuration that must be updated as part of the cluster migration. If a Nexus endpoint still points to the old cluster's gRPC/HTTP frontend and that frontend has been decommissioned or reconfigured to only accept the new CA bundle, callers from the new cluster (presenting new-CA certs) or from the old cluster (presenting old-CA certs to a frontend that no longer exists) will fail at connection time. The production lesson: Nexus endpoint configuration is cluster-topology-aware and must be treated as part of your migration runbook alongside DNS, load balancer, and firewall changes — not just PKI rotation.

questions.question:
Consider the following Temporal Cloud configuration: a namespace uses mTLS for worker connectivity, namespace-level data encryption is implemented via a remote codec server, and the namespace retention period is 30 days with archival enabled to an S3 bucket. A compliance team requires that all workflow execution data be cryptographically inaccessible (key destroyed) within 72 hours of workflow completion for a specific sensitive data class. An engineer proposes: 'We'll rotate the codec server's encryption key every 48 hours and delete old key versions after 72 hours. Temporal Cloud will only store encrypted blobs, so destroying the key achieves crypto-shredding.' What is the critical flaw in this proposal?

A) Temporal Cloud's archival integration re-encodes archived payloads using Temporal-managed encryption before writing to S3, so destroying the codec key leaves a second copy of plaintext data in the archival store that is outside the engineer's key rotation scope.

B) The 30-day namespace retention period means Temporal Cloud retains closed workflow history for 30 days regardless of key rotation; while history bytes remain encrypted, the Search Attribute index in the visibility store may contain unencoded metadata fields that persist for the full retention window beyond the 72-hour key destruction window.

C) Key destruction in the codec server immediately invalidates all in-flight workflow executions that haven't yet completed, because workers cache the current codec key and cannot re-fetch it mid-execution, causing all pending activities to fail with codec decryption errors.

D) Archival to S3 happens asynchronously after workflow close; if a workflow closes within the 48-hour key rotation window, the archival job may run after the key version used to encode that workflow's payloads has already been deleted, writing permanently undecodable blobs to the archival store.

choices.text: Temporal Cloud's archival integration re-encodes archived payloads using Temporal-managed encryption before writing to S3, so destroying the codec key leaves a second copy of plaintext data in the archival store that is outside the engineer's key rotation scope.

The 30-day namespace retention period means Temporal Cloud retains closed workflow history for 30 days regardless of key rotation; while history bytes remain encrypted, the Search Attribute index in the visibility store may contain unencoded metadata fields that persist for the full retention window beyond the 72-hour key destruction window.

Key destruction in the codec server immediately invalidates all in-flight workflow executions that haven't yet completed, because workers cache the current codec key and cannot re-fetch it mid-execution, causing all pending activities to fail with codec decryption errors.

Archival to S3 happens asynchronously after workflow close; if a workflow closes within the 48-hour key rotation window, the archival job may run after the key version used to encode that workflow's payloads has already been deleted, writing permanently undecodable blobs to the archival store.

questions.explanation: This question forces reasoning across Data Converter / codec server crypto-shredding, namespace retention, archival, and the visibility store — all four interacting simultaneously. Option A is wrong: Temporal Cloud (and self-hosted archival) archives the exact payload bytes it has stored — the opaque encrypted blobs from the codec server. Temporal does not re-encrypt or re-encode them. The archived bytes are just as inaccessible as the live history once the key is destroyed. Option C is wrong: codec key rotation is a server-side concern in the codec server; workers call the codec server's encode/decode endpoints at activity boundary time and don't cache raw key material. A well-designed codec server supports key versioning so old payloads remain decodable until keys are explicitly destroyed. Option D describes a real operational hazard with key rotation timing and archival lag, but it's the wrong 'critical flaw' — this is a timing/operational concern that can be mitigated by keeping old key versions alive until archival confirms completion. Option B identifies the architectural gap: Search Attributes written to the visibility store (Elasticsearch/OpenSearch in self-hosted, or Temporal Cloud's visibility layer) are stored as *plaintext indexed fields* because their entire purpose is to be queryable. If engineers store any sensitive data in Search Attributes (common mistake: storing customer IDs, amounts, or status strings), those fields live in the visibility store for the full retention period and are not subject to codec encryption at all. This is the compliance gap: crypto-shredding the codec key destroys payload body content but leaves Search Attribute metadata queryable and readable. The production lesson: never put sensitive regulated data in Search Attributes; treat them as public metadata. Route sensitive fields through workflow input/output payloads that go through the codec.

questions.question:
A team is deploying Temporal on a self-hosted cluster inside a private cloud with strict network segmentation. They configure private link / internal load balancers so worker pods reach the Temporal frontend on an internal endpoint only. mTLS is enabled cluster-wide. They now want to add Temporal's Prometheus metrics endpoint for observability. The security team mandates: 'The metrics endpoint must not be reachable from the same network segment as the worker task queue traffic.' The engineer proposes exposing Temporal Server's metrics port on a separate network interface bound to the ops VLAN, while the gRPC frontend remains on the application VLAN. After deployment, workers start reporting intermittent task queue polling failures during peak load. What is the most likely cause?

A) Binding the metrics HTTP server to a specific interface causes Temporal Server's internal gossip/membership protocol to also rebind to that interface, disrupting inter-node ring membership and causing task queue ownership rebalancing that drops in-flight polls during the rebalancing window.

B) The Prometheus scrape requests from the ops VLAN generate enough connection overhead on the shared Temporal Server process that the gRPC listener's accept queue fills, causing sporadic poll timeouts under peak combined load from both metrics scrapes and worker poll traffic.

C) Temporal Server uses the configured advertise address for both inter-node RPC and the metrics endpoint; splitting them across VLANs causes the server's self-reported address in cluster membership to route to the ops VLAN interface, making other frontend nodes unable to forward requests to this node on the application VLAN.

D) The private link configuration creates an asymmetric routing condition where worker TCP connections established before the interface split complete their TLS handshake on the application VLAN but Temporal Server's response path resolves through the ops VLAN interface, causing TCP RST packets that manifest as poll failures.

choices.text: Binding the metrics HTTP server to a specific interface causes Temporal Server's internal gossip/membership protocol to also rebind to that interface, disrupting inter-node ring membership and causing task queue ownership rebalancing that drops in-flight polls during the rebalancing window.

The Prometheus scrape requests from the ops VLAN generate enough connection overhead on the shared Temporal Server process that the gRPC listener's accept queue fills, causing sporadic poll timeouts under peak combined load from both metrics scrapes and worker poll traffic.

Temporal Server uses the configured advertise address for both inter-node RPC and the metrics endpoint; splitting them across VLANs causes the server's self-reported address in cluster membership to route to the ops VLAN interface, making other frontend nodes unable to forward requests to this node on the application VLAN.

The private link configuration creates an asymmetric routing condition where worker TCP connections established before the interface split complete their TLS handshake on the application VLAN but Temporal Server's response path resolves through the ops VLAN interface, causing TCP RST packets that manifest as poll failures.

questions.explanation: This question tests the interaction between Temporal Server's network topology configuration (advertise address, membership ring), metrics endpoint binding, private link / VLAN segmentation, and mTLS. The critical concept: Temporal Server's cluster membership protocol (used for task queue partition ownership via consistent hashing) requires nodes to advertise a reachable address to their peers. In most Temporal Server configurations, a single `broadcastAddress` or `listenAddress` is used for both the public-facing gRPC endpoint AND the address this node advertises to peers for internal RPC. If a naive interface-split configuration causes the node to advertise its ops VLAN address (perhaps because the metrics HTTP bind is done by changing the primary listen address), other history/matching/frontend nodes on the application VLAN will attempt to route task queue ownership RPCs to an unreachable address, causing ownership rebalancing attempts and dropped polls. Option A is plausible-sounding but wrong: the metrics HTTP server bind is a separate listener that does not affect the gossip/membership protocol bind in Temporal's architecture. Option B (resource exhaustion) is possible in theory but not 'most likely' — Prometheus scrapes are lightweight HTTP GET requests and Temporal's metrics server is a separate HTTP listener, not the gRPC frontend. Option D involves a real networking concern but describes TCP asymmetric routing, which would manifest immediately and consistently, not intermittently under peak load. The production lesson: when segmenting Temporal Server network interfaces for security compliance, carefully audit which address is used as the `broadcastAddress` for cluster membership — it must remain reachable from ALL other Temporal Server nodes on the internal cluster network, regardless of what network changes you make to the observability or client-facing interfaces.
